title: USEC_OPENMSG
html:
USEC_OPENMSG

  • Last UpdatedJan 17, 2023
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

Prepares the messages that are to be sent to the server in order to carry out the initialisation and authentication protocol to establish a connection.

Use

SMTP security drivers

Expected Input

Properties, Parameters

InBuf, InDataLen, InBufLen

DrvArea

Expected Output

Return status

OutBuf, OutDataLen, OutBufLen

ErrMsg, ErrMsgLen

DrvArea

Return Values

USEC_SUCCESS

USEC_INFO

USEC_ERROR

Description

The USEC_OPENMSG function prepares the messages that are to be sent to the server in order to carry out the initialization and authentication protocol to establish a connection. This function is called repeatedly with the server’s responses until the driver indicates that the protocol is complete or has failed.

The parameters specified in the second argument to the SetSecurity operation are intended to be used by the driver at this stage. The driver should now verify that the Parameters member contains valid and useful information. If not, it should return USEC_ERROR with an explanatory message.

Buffers

The driver uses the OutBuf member to deliver its messages and the InBuf member to receive the replies from the SMTP server. These buffers are provided by the caller and their maximum lengths are in OutBufLen and InBufLen respectively. Currently they are 4096 bytes.

The driver may allocate its own buffers if the supplied buffers are too small. In this case, it must set the pointers in OutBuf and InBuf, and set the buffers maximum lengths in OutBufLen and InBufLen respectively. The pointers for the OutBuf and InBuf are not changed by the caller of the API and are available over subsequent driver calls.

The driver is responsible for freeing its own allocated buffers. When the driver has finished with the message exchange, it can free any allocated in or out buffers. The driver prepares a buffer containing a message in the OutBuf member of the driver structure. The length of the message in bytes is set in the OutDataLen member. This buffer is sent to the SMTP server 'as is', so it should conform to the SMTP protocol.

For example, the last two bytes should be CarriageReturn, LineFeed. The driver should return the status USEC_CONTINUE to indicate that the OutBuf should be sent to the SMTP driver. The reply from the SMTP server is placed in the InBuf member and the length of the data will be set in the InDataLen member. This buffer will be exactly as it was sent by the SMTP server with the CarriageReturn, LineFeed at the end. The driver is then called again with this function code to examine the reply of the SMTP server. The driver must determine if another message should be sent to the SMTP server or not. If so, it must repeat the cycle of preparing the OutBuf and returning USEC_CONTINUE. The API does not provide any information about how many times the cycle has been repeated.

The buffer content is the responsibility of the driver in terms of character set and format. The buffers are completely opaque to the caller. The caller simply transports the buffers between the driver and the SMTP server and does not modify the buffers in any way. When the driver returns USEC_CONTINUE, it is instructing the caller to send a message and then receive a message.

The driver can also create other protocols by returning USEC_WRITEONLY or USEC_READONLY, where the OutBuf is written to the server and then the driver is called again, or where only another read from the server into the InBuf is done and the driver is called again.

Returning Status

When the driver has established an authenticated connection, it should return the status USEC_SUCCESS or USEC_INFO to indicate that the connection can be used for sending mail.

A return status of USEC_ERROR means an error has occurred. In this case, no other drivers in the security context will establish a connection. Those that have already established a connection will be called to close the protocol (if they support it), and no mail can be sent.

USEC_INFO and USEC_ERROR both should return an explanatory message pointed to by the ErrMsg member. This message must be null-terminated. The message will only be used if the length of the buffer in ErrMsgLen member is not zero. This allows a pointer to allocated memory to be stored in ErrMsg without it necessarily being printed.
